title: Coptic stitch with hardboard covers
subtitle: A full Coptic-stitch journal with sewn hardboard covers
excerpt: A Coptic-stitch journal with hardboard covers is the binding that opened the exposed-spine aesthetic to the modern bookbinding revival. The first and last signatures sew through the boards; the chain-stitch link at each kettle station pulls both boards and text block into a single structure. The book opens completely flat at every page.
sourceNotes: Coptic-stitch binding is documented historically in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), Internet Archive: chapter on early binding structures. The Coptic name refers to the early Egyptian Christian bindery tradition used from the second century CE onward; the chain-stitch link is described in Cockerell's structural plates. Modern Coptic revival practice (as in Esther K. Smith and Dikko Faust, Making Books, 2003) is in copyright; only structural principles are drawn here.

glossaryTerms.definition: An exposed-spine binding technique where each signature is sewn through the boards and linked to the previous signature with a chain-stitch loop at each sewing station. Named for the early Egyptian Christian binderies. The spine shows the thread links as a decorative pattern and the book opens completely flat.

The linking stitch worked at the head and tail sewing stations of a multi-signature binding. The needle catches the thread of the previous signature before entering the new one, pulling the two signatures together.
A group of sheets folded together to form one section of a book. In a Coptic journal, a 6-sheet signature at 90 gsm gives twelve leaves and twenty-four pages per section.
A pierced hole in a signature or board through which the needle passes. The spacing of sewing stations determines the pattern of chain links on the spine.

In a Coptic binding with boards, the first and last signatures sew through the boards rather than through another signature. This locks the boards to the text block as structural members of the binding, not as separately glued cases.

projectSchedule.title: Fold signatures, cut and cover boards
projectSchedule.body: Fold all signatures and press the folds. Cut the 2 mm boards to signature size. If covering the boards with book cloth, do it now and press under a flat weight.

projectSchedule.title: Pierce stations and sew the binding
projectSchedule.body: Boards have had overnight to dry if covered. Pierce the sewing stations in boards and all signatures. Sew the full binding from first board through the text block to the back board.

content.text: The
Coptic stitch

is named for the early Egyptian Christian binderies that used it from the second century CE onward. The chain-stitch link is visible on the outside of the spine, which is left uncovered. This tutorial makes a full journal with hardboard covers: the first and last
signatures

sew through the boards, anchoring them as part of the structure. The finished book opens completely flat at any page. Paper weight: 90 gsm, grain short (grain parallel to the short edge of the folded signature, which means grain parallel to the spine). Finished size: A5 (148 mm × 210 mm), 6 signatures of 6 sheets each.

items.name: Bookbinding thread, linen (waxed)
items.qty: approx. 10× spine length for the full text block

items.name: Text paper, 90 gsm, grain short
items.substitutions: Any 80–100 gsm cartridge at correct grain direction

items.name: Bookbinding board, 2 mm (2 pieces)
items.notes: Cut to same dimensions as a folded signature: 148 mm × 210 mm for A5

items.name: Book cloth for covering boards (optional)
items.qty: enough for both boards with 15 mm turn-in on all four edges

items.name: Book press (optional, for pressing covered boards)

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones: blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, never in front of it. Cut away from the body. Bookbinding adhesives: PVA is safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Clean brushes with warm water immediately after use.

content.text: Preparing the signatures

Fold each group of 6 sheets individually and press the fold with the bone folder. A 6-sheet signature at 90 gsm folds to a spine thickness of roughly 2 mm, which suits the 2 mm board covers. Nest all 6 sheets into one signature and run the bone folder along the spine fold again to consolidate. Make 6 signatures this way.
Cutting and covering the boards

Cut two pieces of 2 mm bookbinding board to 148 mm × 210 mm, with grain direction parallel to the 210 mm (spine) dimension. Check the grain by flexing the board gently: it bends more easily along the grain. The board cut against grain will warp when paste or adhesive is applied.

If covering the boards with book cloth: cut the cloth 30 mm larger on all four sides. Apply a thin, even coat of PVA to the board face with the paste brush. Press the board cloth-side down, centred. Fold the turn-ins with mitred corners. Press under the book press or under a flat weight overnight. The boards must be completely dry before piercing the sewing stations.
Marking and drilling sewing stations
Mark 5
sewing stations

on the spine edge of one signature: 10 mm from head (station 1), 10 mm from tail (station 5), and three evenly spaced between them. All stations sit 7 mm from the spine edge. Transfer this spacing to all 6 signatures and both boards by stacking and marking through with the awl tip.

Pierce all station holes in the signatures from the inside of the fold outward. The awl should exit cleanly on the spine face. For the boards, pierce straight through from the inside face to the outside face at each station. Board holes need a firm push and a slight twist of the awl to cut through the board cleanly.
Sewing the binding

Thread a length of waxed linen about ten times the spine length. The sewing sequence starts with the front board. Hold the front board inside-face up (the same orientation it will have in the finished book). The first signature goes on top of the board, with its fold toward the head.

Enter station 1 (head) from inside the first signature, through the signature and the board together, and exit on the outside face of the board. Leave a 60 mm tail inside. Sew from head to tail through the board and signature at each station. At the tail station (station 5), work a
kettle stitch
: loop behind the exit thread and pull through to lock. Then enter the second signature.

For signature 2 and all subsequent middle signatures: sew tail to head (alternating direction). At each interior station, after exiting the spine of the current signature, pass the needle under the corresponding thread link on the previous signature before re-entering the same hole. This is the
board attachment
link for the board signatures; for middle signatures, it is the standard Coptic chain link. Work a kettle stitch at each end station before entering the next signature.

The last signature sews through both the final signature and the back board together, mirroring the first sequence. Finish with two half-hitches around the tail-station thread and trim the working thread to 5 mm.
Finishing

The finished journal needs no further adhesive work. Press the text block under a flat weight for an hour if the signatures do not lie uniformly flat. The spine should show a clean row of chain links running head to tail with even spacing. If any link has slipped to the side, it was pulled unevenly during sewing; this is cosmetic only and does not affect the binding's strength.
Care

Store the journal upright on a shelf with books on either side to support the covers. A Coptic-bound journal with exposed boards is more prone to corner damage than a cased book; the corners can be reinforced with small triangles of book cloth pressed on with PVA if needed. The binding opens flat at any page and does not need to be forced.
Variations

A two-colour thread binding: use a light thread for the interior signatures and a contrasting colour for the board-attachment signatures. The colour change is visible on the spine at the two junction points and reads as a deliberate design choice.

A limp-cover Coptic (no boards, the first and last signatures act as the covers) is the simplest Coptic structure to make. The same sewing sequence applies; the first and last signatures simply use heavier paper (200-250 gsm) instead of board.
